era_context: The Roman Empire in the early 4th century CE, under the Tetrarchy established by Diocletian (284–305 CE). The empire is administratively divided into four regions under two Augusti and two Caesares. Christianity is still a persecuted minority religion, though Diocletian's Great Persecution (303–311 CE) is underway. In the East, the Sassanid Persian Empire under Hormizd II controls the Middle East and Central Asia. China is fragmented during the Western Jin Dynasty's decline, with regional warlords rising in power. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe remain outside the documentary record of Mediterranean and East Asian sources.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological and no contemporary written sources)
Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written records in European tradition; Maya Classic period ongoing but undated at year-level precision)
Southeast Asia (limited contemporary sources in Western archives)

events.title: Diocletian's Great Persecution of Christians intensifies
events.description: Diocletian's systematic persecution of Christians, initiated by edict in 303 CE, continues with renewed intensity in 300 CE and beyond. Christians are required to sacrifice to pagan gods; refusal results in execution, torture, or enslavement. The persecution is most severe in the Eastern provinces under Diocletian's direct control. This represents the most organized, empire-wide suppression of Christianity before Constantine's conversion. The persecution will ultimately fail to eradicate Christianity and may have strengthened Christian identity through martyrdom narratives.
key_figures: Diocletian (Roman Emperor, Augustus of the East)
Galerius (Caesar under Diocletian, advocate for persecution)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine and Ecclesiastical History
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors
Acts of Christian martyrs (various, compiled later)

events.certainty_note: The Great Persecution is well-attested in multiple Christian sources written within a generation of events. Diocletian's edicts are referenced in later legal compilations. However, no contemporary pagan sources survive to corroborate details, and Christian accounts may emphasize severity for theological purposes.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty fragmentation accelerates in China
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE), which briefly unified China after the Three Kingdoms period, is rapidly fragmenting due to the War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE). Regional military governors (jiedushi) and noble families accumulate power independent of the central court. By 300 CE, the Jin court's authority is severely weakened, and the empire is effectively divided into competing regional powers. This fragmentation will lead to the period of the Sixteen Kingdoms and the Northern and Southern Dynasties, lasting until reunification under the Tang Dynasty in 618 CE.
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (reigning but politically weak)
Various princes and regional warlords competing for power

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang (11th century)
Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled in 7th century

events.certainty_note: The War of the Eight Princes and Jin fragmentation are well-documented in Chinese historical records, though these were compiled centuries later. The broad outline is accepted by modern scholarship, though specific dates and details of individual campaigns may vary.

events.title: Hormizd II rules Sassanid Persia
events.description: Hormizd II (r. 302–309 CE, though some sources date his reign 309–379 CE with confusion over regnal years) rules the Sassanid Persian Empire at the height of its power. He consolidates control over the eastern frontier against nomadic invasions and maintains the western border against Roman encroachment. Hormizd II is remembered in later Sassanid tradition as a strong administrator, though contemporary sources are limited. His reign represents a period of relative stability for the Sassanid state before internal succession disputes.

sources.name: Shahnameh (Book of Kings), Persian epic compiled by Firdawsi (10th century)

events.certainty_note: Hormizd II's reign is attested in Sassanid tradition and later Persian epics, but contemporary Sassanid written sources are sparse. Roman sources provide some corroboration of his military activities. Dating and length of reign are debated among scholars; some sources place him later (309–379 CE) with different regnal numbering.

events.title: Tetrarchic administrative system functions across Roman Empire
events.description: Diocletian's Tetrarchy—dividing the empire into four administrative zones under two Augusti (senior emperors) and two Caesares (junior emperors)—is in full operation. Diocletian rules the Eastern provinces from Nicomedia; Maximian rules the Western provinces from Milan. The system is designed to improve administrative efficiency and military response across the vast empire. However, tensions between the four rulers and ambitions for sole power will eventually destabilize the system, leading to civil wars after Diocletian's abdication in 305 CE.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors

events.certainty_note: The Tetrarchic system is well-documented in contemporary inscriptions and later historical accounts. The administrative structure and the names and roles of the four rulers are firmly established.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Hormizd II's reign is disputed: some modern scholars place his rule in the 4th century CE (309–379 CE) rather than 302–309 CE, creating uncertainty about whether he was ruling in 300 CE at all. The severity and scope of Diocletian's persecution of Christians is debated; some scholars argue that persecution was less systematic and widespread than Christian sources suggest, and that Christian accounts may have exaggerated the scale for theological and martyrological purposes. The Tetrarchy's effectiveness is also questioned by some historians, who note that tensions between the four rulers were present from the outset and that the system was inherently unstable.
historiographic_note: The year 300 CE is well-documented for the Roman Empire and China, with multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian civilizations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have virtually no written records from this period in the Western tradition, though archaeological evidence exists. Sassanid Persia is less well-documented than the Roman Empire, with most sources being later compilations or foreign accounts. The surviving sources are often biased toward court-sponsored narratives and may underrepresent dissent, regional variation, and the experiences of non-elite populations.

graph_edges.note: Diocletian's persecution continues until Galerius issues an edict of toleration in 311 CE
Fragmentation of Western Jin accelerates, leading to formal collapse of the dynasty
Tetrarchic system destabilizes after Diocletian's abdication in 305 CE
